glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of folding and layering two or more colours of polymer clay together to create gradients or mixed-colour effects.
Polymer clay with a clear or semi-clear base that allows light to pass through after curing, used to create depth effects when layered with coloured clay.
The process of hardening polymer clay by baking it in a domestic oven at the temperature specified by the manufacturer, typically 110-130 degrees C.

content.text: Tortoiseshell is one of the most effective faux finishes in polymer clay because the streaked amber-and-brown pattern comes directly from the
colour blending
process itself. There is no painting involved. The key is knowing when to stop blending: too few folds and the colours sit in separate blobs; too many and they merge into muddy brown.
Translucent clay
in the mix adds the depth that makes the finished piece look like it has layers underneath the surface. This is a decorative piece only and is not food-safe.

materials.name: Polymer clay, amber or golden yellow
Eye pin or head pin for pendant loop
Jump ring and necklace cord or chain

tools.name: Pottery rolling pin or smooth glass bottle
Needle tool or craft knife for trimming
Baking tray lined with greaseproof paper

content.text: Preparation

Warm all three clay colours in your hands for 2-3 minutes each until they are soft and pliable. Cold clay tears rather than blends. Preheat your oven to 110-130 degrees C (check the clay packaging for the exact figure) and allow it to reach temperature before
curing
. Line a small baking tray with greaseproof paper.

Tear the amber clay into four rough pieces. Flatten each piece between your palms into a disc about 5 mm thick. Stack the four amber discs with a thin streak of dark brown clay between each layer, and a thin sheet of translucent clay on the outside of the stack. You are building a loose layered block, not blending yet.

Fold the whole stack in half and press together. Fold in half again. Do not twist: you want streaks running roughly parallel, not spiralling. After four folds, pull the clay lengthwise to stretch the streaks thinner. Fold once more. Stop at six to eight total folds. The colours should look like light and dark amber streaks rather than one uniform colour.

Roll the blended clay into a ball, then press flat with your palm to roughly 5 mm thickness. Roll lightly with the rolling pin to even it out to about 4 mm, avoiding heavy pressure that would blur the streaks.

Cut a pendant shape from the rolled slab. An oval roughly 35 x 25 mm works well. An abstract teardrop or rounded rectangle are equally effective. Cut with the needle tool or craft knife, pulling steadily rather than sawing.

Press the eye pin or head pin gently into the top edge of the pendant until roughly 5 mm protrudes inside the clay body. The pin needs to be embedded deep enough to hold the weight of the pendant after curing. Smooth any clay displaced around the pin entry point.

Place the pendant on the lined baking tray and bake at 110-130 degrees C for 20-25 minutes. Once baked, leave in the oven with the door ajar until fully cooled; removing polymer clay while hot can cause brittleness.
Once cool, attach the jump ring to the eye pin loop and thread the cord or chain through. The pendant is complete and ready to wear as a decorative piece.
Variations
Add a small amount of metallic gold clay to the amber base for a richer shimmer effect.
Make matching earring drops by pressing smaller rounds from the same blended slab before curing.
Substitute green and black clay for the amber and brown to create a faux malachite variation.
Sand with fine wet-and-dry paper (400 then 800 grit) after curing for a polished stone-like finish.

items.problem: Colours blended into a uniform brown with no streaks
items.fix: Start again with fresh clay. Stop at no more than eight folds and check the pattern before folding again.

items.problem: Eye pin falls out after curing
items.cause: Pin not embedded deep enough into the clay body
items.fix: For a fresh piece, push the pin at least 8 mm into the clay. For a finished piece where the pin has pulled out, use strong jewellery adhesive to re-seat it.

items.problem: Pendant is brittle and snaps at thin edges
items.cause: Oven temperature too high or clay underbaked and then cooled too rapidly
items.fix: Check oven calibration with a separate thermometer. Bake at 110 degrees C for a longer time rather than higher temperature, and cool slowly inside the oven.
